author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

ccel.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

title: Psalm XC

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This Psalm is entitled, “The prayer of Moses the man of God,” through whom, His man, God gave the law to His people, through whom He freed them from the house of slavery, and led them forty years through the wilderness.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Moses was therefore the Minister of the Old, and the Prophet of the New Testament.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For “all these things,” saith the Apostle, “happened unto them for ensamples: and they are written for our admonition, unto whom the ends of the world come.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In accordance therefore with this dispensation which was vouchsafed to Moses, this Psalm is to be examined, as it has received its title from his prayer.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Lord,” he saith, “Thou hast been our refuge from one generation to another” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 1): either in every generation, or in two generations, the old and new: because, as I said, he was the Minister of the Testament that related to the old generation, and the Prophet of the Testament which appertained to the new.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Jesus Himself, the Surety of that covenant, and the Bridegroom in the marriage which He entered into in that generation, saith, “Had ye believed Moses, ye would have believed Me: for he wrote of Me.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now it is not to be believed that this Psalm was entirely the composition of that Moses, as it is not distinguished by any of those of his expressions which are used in his songs: but the name of the great servant of God is used for the sake of some intimation, which should direct the attention of the reader or listener.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Lord,” he saith, “Thou hast been our refuge from one generation to the other.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He adds, how He became our refuge, since He began to be that, viz. a refuge, to us which He had not been before, not that He had not existed before He became our refuge: “Before the mountains were brought forth, or ever the earth and the world were made: and from age even unto age Thou art” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Thou therefore who art for ever, and before we were, and before the world was, hast become our refuge ever since we turned to Thee.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But the expression, “before the mountains,” etc., seems to me to contain a particular meaning; for mountains are the higher parts of the earth, and if God was before even the earth were formed (or, as some books have it, from the same Greek word, “framed”), since it was by Him that it was formed, what is the need of saying that He was before the mountains, or any certain parts of it, since God was not only before the earth, but before heaven and earth, and even the whole bodily and spiritual creation?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But it may certainly be that the whole rational creation is marked by this distinction; that while the loftiness of Angels is signified by the mountains, the lowliness of man is meant by the earth.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And for this reason, although all the works of creation are not improperly said to be either made or formed; nevertheless, if there is any propriety in these words, the Angels are “made;” for as they are enumerated among His heavenly works, the enumeration itself is thus concluded: “He spake the word, and they were made; He commanded, and they were created;” but the earth was “formed,” that man might thence be created in the body.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For the Scripture uses this word, where we read, God made, or “God formed man out of the dust of the ground.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Before then the noblest parts of the creation (for what is higher than the rational part of the Heavenly creation) were made: before the earth was made, that Thou mightest have worshippers upon the earth; and even this is little, as all these had a beginning either in or with time; but “from age to age Thou art.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It would have been better, from everlasting to everlasting: for God, who is before the ages, exists not from a certain age, nor to a certain age, which has an end, since He is without end.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But it often happens in the Scripture, that the equivocal Greek word causes the Latin translator to put age for eternity and eternity for age.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But he very rightly does not say, Thou wast from ages, and unto ages Thou shalt be: but puts the verb in the present, intimating that the substance of God is altogether immutable.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is not, He was, and Shall be, but only Is.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whence the expression, I Am that I Am; and, I Am “hath sent me unto you;” and, “Thou shalt change them, and they shall be changed: but Thou art the same, and Thy years shall not fail.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Behold then the eternity that is our refuge, that we may fly thither from the mutability of time, there to remain for evermore.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But as our life here is exposed to numerous and great temptations, and it is to be feared lest we may be turned aside by them from that refuge, let us see what in consequence of this the prayer of the man of God seeks for.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Turn not Thou man to lowness” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 3): that is, let not man, turned aside from Thy eternal and sublime things, lust for things of time, savour of earthly things.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This prayer is what God has Himself enjoined us, in the Prayer, “Lead us not into temptation,” He adds, “Again Thou sayest, Come again, ye children of men.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: As if he said, I ask of Thee what Thou hast commanded me to ask: giving glory to His grace, that “he that glorieth, in the Lord he may glory:” without whose help we cannot by an exertion of our own will overcome the temptations of this life.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Turn not Thou man to lowness: again thou sayest, Turn again, ye children of men.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But grant what Thou has enjoined, by hearing the prayer of him who can at least pray, and aiding the faith of the willing soul.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For a thousand years in Thy sight are but as yesterday, which is past by” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 4): hence we ought to turn to Thy refuge, where Thou art without any change, from the fleeting scenes around us; since however long a time may be wished for for this life, “a thousand years in Thy sight are but as yesterday:” not as to-morrow, which is to come: for all limited periods of time are reckoned as having already passed.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Hence the Apostle’s choice is rather to aim at what is before, that is, to desire things eternal, and to forget things behind, by which temporal matters should be understood.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But that no one may imagine a thousand years are reckoned by God as one day, as if with God days were so long, when this is only said in contempt of the extent of time: he adds, “and as a watch in the night:” which only lasts three hours.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Nevertheless men have ventured to assert their knowledge of times, to the pretenders to which our Lord said, “It is not for you to know the times or seasons, which the Father hath put in His own power:” and they allege that this period may be defined six thousand years, as of six days.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Nor have they heeded the words, “are but as one day which is past by:” for, when this was uttered, not a thousand years only had passed, and the expression, “as a watch in the night,” ought to have warned them that they might not be deceived by the uncertainty of the seasons: for even if the six first days in which God finished His works seemed to give some plausibility to their opinion, six watches, which amount to eighteen hours, will not consist with that opinion.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Next, the man of God, or rather the Prophetic spirit, seems to be reciting some law written in the secret wisdom of God, in which He has fixed a limit to the sinful life of mortals, and determined the troubles of mortality, in the following words: “Their years are as things which are nothing worth: in the morning let it fade away like the grass” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The happiness therefore of the heirs of the old covenant, which they asked of the Lord their God as a great boon, attained to receive this Law in His mysterious Providence.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Moses seems to be reciting it: “Their years shall be things which are esteemed as nothing.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Such are those things which are not before they are come: and when come, shall soon not be: for they do not come to be here, but to be gone.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “In the morning,” that is, before they come, “as a heat let it pass by;” but “in the evening,” it means after they come, “let it fall, and be dried up, and withered” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is “to fall” in death, be “dried up” in the corpse, “withered” in the dust.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What is this but flesh, wherein is the accursed lust of fleshly things?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For all flesh is grass, and all the goodliness of man as the flower of the field; the grass withereth, the flower fadeth: but the word of the Lord abideth for ever.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Making no secret that this fate is a penalty inflicted for sin, he adds at once, “For we consume away in Thy displeasure, and are troubled at Thy wrathful indignation” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 7): we consume away in our weakness, and are troubled from the fear of death; for we are become weak, and yet fearful to end that weakness.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Another,” saith He, “shall gird thee, and carry thee whither thou wouldest not:” although not to be punished, but to be crowned, by martyrdom; and the soul of our Lord, transforming us into Himself, was sorrowful even unto death: for “the Lord’s going out” is no other than in “death.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Thou hast set our misdeeds before Thee” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 8): that is, Thou hast not dissembled Thine anger: “and our age in the light of Thy countenance.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “The light of Thy countenance” answers to “before Thee,” and to “our misdeeds,” as above.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For all our days are failed, and in Thine anger we have failed” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: These words sufficiently prove that our subjection to death is a punishment.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He speaks of our days failing, either because men fail in them from loving things that pass away, or because they are reduced to so small a number; which he asserts in the following lines: “our years are spent in thought like a spider.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “The days of our age are threescore years and ten; and though men be so strong that they come to fourscore years, yet is more of them but labour and sorrow” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: These words appear to express the shortness and misery of this life: since those who have reached their seventieth year are styled old men.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Up to eighty, however, they appear to have some strength; but if they live beyond this, their existence is laborious through multiplied sorrows.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Yet many even below the age of seventy experience an old age the most infirm and wretched: and old men have often been found to be wonderfully vigorous even beyond eighty years.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is therefore better to search for some spiritual meaning in these numbers.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For the anger of God is not greater on the sins of Adam (through whom alone “sin entered into the world, and death by sin, and so death passed upon all men”), because they live a much shorter time than the men of old; since even the length of their days is ridiculed in the comparison of a thousand years to yesterday that is past, and to three hours: especially since at the very time when they provoked the anger of God to send the deluge in which they perished, their life was at its longest span.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Moreover, seventy and eighty years equal a hundred and fifty; a number which the Psalms clearly insinuate to be a sacred one.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: One hundred and fifty have the same relative signification as fifteen, the latter number being composed of seven and eight together: the first of which points to the Old Testament through the observation of the Sabbath; the latter to the New, referring to the resurrection of our Lord.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Hence the fifteen steps in the Temple.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Hence in the Psalms, fifteen “songs of degrees.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Hence the waters of the deluge overtopped the highest mountains by fifteen cubits: and many other instances of the same nature.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Our years are passed in thought like a spider.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: We were labouring in things corruptible, corruptible works were we weaving together: which, as the Prophet Isaiah saith, by no means covered us.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “The days of our years are in themselves,” etc. A distinction is here made between themselves and their strength: “in themselves,” that is, in the years or days themselves, may mean in temporal things, which are promised in the Old Testament, signified by the number seventy; “but if” not in themselves, but “in their strength,” refers not to temporal things, but to things eternal, “fourscore years,” as the New Testament contains the hope of a new life and resurrection for evermore: and what is added, that if they pass this latter period, “their strength is labour and sorrow,” intimates that such shall be the fate of him who goes beyond this faith, and seeks for more.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It may also be understood thus: because although we are established in the New Testament, which the number eighty signifies, yet still our life is one of labour and sorrow, while “we groan within ourselves, awaiting the adoption, to wit, the redemption of our body; for we are saved by hope; and if we hope for that we see not, then do we with patience wait for it.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This relates to the mercy of God, of which he proceeds to say, “Since thy mercy cometh over us, and we shall be chastened:” for “the Lord chasteneth whom He loveth, and scourgeth every son whom He receiveth,” and to some mighty ones He giveth a thorn in the flesh, to buffet them, that they may not be exalted above measure through the abundance of the revelations, so that strength be made perfect in weakness.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Some copies read, we shall be “taught,” instead of “chastened,” which is equally expressive of the Divine Mercy; for no man can be taught without labour and sorrow; since strength is made perfect in weakness.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For who knoweth the power of Thy wrath: and for the fear of Thee to number Thine anger?” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It belongs to very few men, he saith, to know the power of Thy wrath; for when Thou dost spare, Thy anger is so far heavier against most men; that we may know that labour and sorrow belong not to wrath, but rather to Thy mercy, when Thou chastenest and teachest those whom Thou lovest, to save them from the torments of eternal punishment: as it is said in another Psalm, “The sinner hath provoked the Lord: He will not require it of him according to the greatness of His wrath.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: With this also is understood, “Who knoweth?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Such is the difficulty of finding any one who knoweth how to number Thine anger by Thy fear, that he adds this, meaning that it is to the purpose that Thou appearest to spare some, with whom Thou art more angry, that the sinner may be prospered in his path, and receive a heavier doom at the last.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For when the power of human wrath hath killed the body, it hath nothing more to do: but God hath power both to punish here, and after the death of the body to send into Hell, and by the few who are thus taught, the vain and seductive prosperity of the wicked is judged to be greater wrath of God.…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Make Thy right hand so well known” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This is the reading of most of the Greek copies: not of some in Latin, which is thus, “Make Thy right hand well known to me.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What is, “Thy right hand,” but Thy Christ, of whom it is said, And to whom is the Arm of the Lord revealed?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Make Him so well known, that Thy faithful may learn in Him to ask and to hope for those things rather of Thee as rewards of their faith, which do not appear in the Old Testament, but are revealed in the New: that they may not imagine that the happiness derived from earthly and temporal blessings is to be highly esteemed, desired, or loved, and thus their feet slip, when they see it in men who honour Thee not: that their steps may not give way, while they know not how to number Thine anger.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Finally, in accordance with this prayer of the Man that is His, He has made His Christ so well known as to show by His sufferings that not these rewards which seem so highly prized in the Old Testament, where they are shadows of things to come, but things eternal, are to be desired.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The right hand of God may also be understood in this sense, as that by which He will separate His saints from the wicked: because that hand becomes well known, when it scourgeth every son whom He receiveth, and suffers him not, in greater anger, to prosper in his sins, but in His mercy scourgeth him with the left, that He may place him purified on His right hand.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The reading of most copies, “make Thy right hand well known to me,” may be referred either to Christ, or to eternal happiness: for God has not a right hand in bodily shape, as He has not that anger which is aroused into violent passion.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But what he addeth, “and those fettered in heart in wisdom;” other copies read, “instructed,” not “lettered:” the Greek verb, expressing both senses, only differing by a single syllable.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But since these also, as it is said, put their “feet in the fetters” of wisdom, are taught wisdom (he means the feet of the heart, not of the body), and bound by its golden chains depart not from the path of God, and become not runaways from him; whichever reading we adopt, the truth in the meaning is safe.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Them thus lettered, or instructed in heart in wisdom, God makes so well known in the New Testament, that they despised all things for the Faith which the impiety of Jews and Gentiles abhorred; and allowed themselves to be deprived of those things which in the Old Testament are thought high promises by those who judge after the flesh.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And as when they became so well known, as to despise these things, and by setting their affections on things eternal, gave a testimony through their sufferings (whence they are called witnesses or martyrs in the Greek), they endured for a long while many bitter temporal afflictions.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This man of God giveth heed to this, and the prophetic spirit under the name of Moses continues thus, “Return, O Lord, how long? and be softened concerning Thy servants” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: These are the words of those, who, enduring many evils in that persecuting age, become known because their hearts are bound in the chain of wisdom so firmly, that not even such hardships can induce them to fly from their Lord to the good things of this world.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “How long wilt Thou hide Thy face from me, O Lord?” occurs in another Psalm, in unison with this sentence, “Return, O Lord, how long?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And that they who, in a most carnal spirit, ascribe to God the form of a human body, may know that the “turning away” and “turning again” of His countenance is not like those motions of our own frame, let them recollect these words from above in the same Psalm, “Thou hast set our misdeeds before Thee, and our secret sins in the light of Thy countenance.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: How then does he say in this passage, “Return,” that God may be favourable, as if He had turned away His face in anger; when as in the former he speaks of God’s anger in such a manner, as to insinuate that He had not turned away His countenance from the misdeeds and the course of life of those He was angry with, but rather had set them before Him, and in the light of His countenance?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The word, “How long,” belongs to righteousness beseeching, not indignant impatience.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Be softened,” some have rendered by a verb, “soften.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But “be softened” avoids an ambiguity; since to soften is a common verb: for he may be said to soften who pours out prayers, and he to whom they are poured out: for we say, I soften thee, and I soften toward thee.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Next, in anticipation of future blessings, of which he speaks as already vouchsafed, he says, “We are satisfied with Thy mercy in the morning” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Prophecy has thus been kindled for us, in the midst of these toils and sorrows of the night, like a lamp in the darkness, until day dawn, and the Day-star arise in our hearts.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For blessed are the pure in heart, for they shall see God: then shall the righteous be filled with that blessing for which they hunger and thirst now, while, walking in faith, they are absent from the Lord.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Hence are the words, “In Thy presence is fulness of joy:” and, “Early in the morning they shall stand by, and shall look up:” and as other translators have said it, “We shall be satisfied with Thy mercy in the morning;” then they shall be satisfied.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: As he says elsewhere, “I shall be satisfied, when Thy glory shall be revealed.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: So it is said, “Lord, show us the Father, and it sufficeth us:” and our Lord Himself answereth, “I will manifest Myself to Zion;” and until this promise is fulfilled, no blessing satisfies us, or ought to do so, lest our longings should be arrested in their course, when they ought to be increased until they gain their objects.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And we rejoiced and were glad all the days of our life.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Those days are days without end: they all exist together: it is thus they satisfy us: for they give not way to days succeeding: since there is nothing there which exists not yet because it has not reached us, or ceases to exist because it has passed; all are together: because there is one day only, which remains and passes not away: this is eternity itself.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: These are the days respecting which it is written, “What man is he that lusteth to live, and would fain see good days?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: These days in another passage are styled years: where unto God it is said, “But Thou art the same, and Thy years shall not fail:” for these are not years that are accounted for nothing, or days that perish like a shadow: but they are days which have a real existence, the number of which he who thus spoke, “Lord, let me know mine end” (that is, after reaching what term I shall remain unchanged, and have no further blessing to crave), “and the number of my days, what it is” (what is, not what is not): prayed to know.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He distinguishes them from the days of this life, of which he speaks as follows, “Behold, Thou hast made my days as it were a span long,” which are not, because they stand not, remain not, but change in quick succession: nor is there a single hour in them in which our being is not such, but that one part of it has already passed, another is about to come, and none remains as it is.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But those years and days, in which we too shall never fail, but evermore be refreshed, will never fail.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Let our souls long earnestly for those days, let them thirst ardently for them, that there we may be filled, be satisfied, and say what we now say in anticipation, “We have been satisfied,” etc. “We have been comforted again now, after the time that Thou hast brought us low, and for the years wherein we have seen evil” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But now in days that are as yet evil, let us speak as follows.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Look upon Thy servants, and upon Thy works” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For Thy servants themselves are Thy works, not only inasmuch as they are men, but as Thy servants, that is, obedient to Thy commands.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For we are His workmanship, created not merely in Adam, but in Christ Jesus, unto good works, which God hath before ordained that we should walk in them: “for it is God which worketh in us both to will and to do of His good pleasure.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And direct their sons:” that they may be right in heart, for to such God is bountiful; for “God is bountiful to Israel, to those that are right in heart.”…

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And let the brightness of the Lord our God be upon us” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 17); whence the words, “O Lord, the light of Thy countenance is marked upon us.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And, “Make Thou straight the works of our hands upon us:” that we may do them not for hope of earthly reward: for then they are not straight, but crooked.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In many copies the Psalm goes thus far, but in some there is found an additional verse at the end, as follows, “And make straight the work of our hands.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: To these words the learned have prefixed a star, called an asterisk, to show that they are found in the Hebrew, or in some other Greek translations, but not in the Septuagint.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The meaning of this verse, if we are to expound it, appears to me this, that all our good works are one work of love: for love is the fulfilling of the Law.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For as in the former verse he had said, “And the works of our hands make Thou straight upon us,” here he says “work,” not works, as if anxious to show, in the last verse, that all our works are one, that is, are directed with a view to one work.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For then are works righteous, when they are directed to this one end: “for the end of the commandment is charity out of a pure heart, and of a good conscience, and of faith unfeigned.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: There is therefore one work, in which are all, “faith which worketh by love:” whence our Lord’s words in the Gospel, “This is the work of God, that ye believe in Him whom He hath sent.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Since, therefore, in this Psalm, both old and new life, life both mortal and everlasting, years that are counted for nought, and years that have the fulness of loving-kindness and of true joy, that is, the penalty of the first and the reign of the Second Man, are marked so very clearly; I imagine, that the name of Moses, the man of God, became the title of the Psalm, that pious and right-minded readers of the Scriptures might gain an intimation that the Mosaic laws, in which God appears to promise only, or nearly only, earthly rewards for good works, without doubt contains under a veil some such hopes as this Psalm displays.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But when any one has passed over to Christ, the veil will be taken away: and his eyes will be unveiled, that he may consider the wonderful things in the law of God, by the gift of Him, to whom we pray, “Open Thou mine eyes, and I shall see the wondrous things of Thy law.
